verses.text: « Par le désir, un homme, s'étant séparé, cherche et se mêle de toute sagesse. » Le verset est célèbre pour sa difficulté de traduction. L'hébreu suggère quelqu'un qui s'isole puis poursuit seulement ses propres désirs — un portrait de l'indépendance complaisante. La sagesse dans les Proverbes n'est pas une poursuite solitaire ; elle est communautaire, apprise en présence des autres.

« Un sot ne prend point plaisir à l'intelligence. » L'hébreu *tebunah* — discernement — nomme le travail patient de saisir ce qui est vrai. Le sot préfère l'expression de soi à la compréhension. Le proverbe coupe encore. Souvent nous savons que nous avons cessé d'apprendre quand nous commençons à jouir de nos propres opinions plus que de la recherche de la vérité.

« Quand le méchant vient, vient aussi le mépris. » Trois choses voyagent ensemble dans ce verset : méchanceté, mépris et opprobre. L'hébreu *bouz* — mépris — nomme le dédain corrosif qui arrive souvent là où la méchanceté s'installe. Le proverbe cartographie les conséquences sociales du mal. Là où il va, le mépris pour ce qui est bon suit.
Les mots sortent et « émergent » de la bouche d’une personne, tout comme l'eau jaillit de la terre (voir aussi **10.20** ; **12.23** ; **16.23** ; cf. **Jc 3.10-12** ).

18:5 It is not good to accept the person of the wicked,.... For a judge to have respect to a wicked man in a cause before him, and to favour him, because he is a rich man, or a relation, or he has received some kindness from him; none of these things should have any influence upon him to overthrow the righteous in judgment: though he may be a poor man and a stranger, and to whom the judge is under no private and personal obligation;

18:6 A fool's lips enter into contention,.... That is, between others, when he has nothing to do with it; but he must be meddling, and make himself a party in the contention, which is an argument of his folly; he says things which occasion disputes, raise contentions among men, and provoke to wrath and anger. The Septuagint version is, "the lips of a fool lead him to evils": for, as they lead him to contention and strife, the issue of that is confusion and every evil work;

18:7 A fool's mouth is his destruction,.... The cause of it; for his contentions, and quarrels, and evil speaking, lawsuits are commenced against him, which bring ruin upon himself and his family now; as well as for his idle and wicked words he will be condemned hereafter, Matt 12:35; there is a world of iniquity in the mouth and tongue of a wicked man, which bring destruction upon himself and others, Jas 3:6; and his lips are the snare of his soul;
Les paroles du rapporteur sont comme des friandises (Cf. **26.22** ) : Les rumeurs peuvent être délicieuses à entendre et à répéter, mais elles sont destructrices.

18:9 He also that is slothful in his work,.... Remiss in it; hangs down his hands, and does not care to make use of them, but neglects his business: is brother to him that is a great waster: a prodigal man, who spends his substance in riotous living: the sluggard and the prodigal are brethren in iniquity; for, though they take different courses, they are both sinful, and issue in the same manner; both bring to poverty and want. Or, "brother to a master that wastes" (p);

Dans un monde troublé, seul l’Éternel peut offrir le salut. Les riches peuvent penser que leur richesse constitue une protection solide, mais le pouvoir de l’argent reste limité ( **11.4** ; **13.8** ).

Dans un monde troublé, seul l’Éternel peut offrir le salut. Les riches peuvent penser que leur richesse constitue une protection solide, mais le pouvoir de l’argent reste limité ( **11.4** ; **13.8** ).

L'arrogance alimente l'orgueil et conduit à l’échec, car elle refuse d’accepter la critique et de se remettre en question. En revanche, l'humilité permet d’apprendre des autres et mène à l’honneur et au succès.

18:13 He that answereth a matter before he heareth it,.... Who is impatient, and cannot wait to hear it out, but breaks in upon the speaker before he has finished what he has to say; or is rash and precipitate, and returns an answer at once, without weighing and considering, and thoroughly understanding, what is said: it is folly and shame unto him; his answer must be a foolish one, and bring shame and confusion upon him; men should be "swift to hear", and "slow to speak", Jas 1:9.
Un problème spirituel ou émotionnel, comme un esprit brisé, peut être bien plus accablant qu'une maladie physique.

18:15 The heart of the prudent getteth knowledge,.... More knowledge; for he must have some, and a considerable share, to be denominated "prudent", whether in things natural, civil, or spiritual: and such will be heartily desirous of more, and make use of all means to attain it, by which they do come at a large share of it; and the ear of the wise seeketh knowledge; a wise man seeks to get it by hearing; he listens to what others say, and especially such that are wiser and more knowing than himself: so such as are wise to salvation, as they desire to know more of Christ and of divine things, and make use of all means for that purpose;
Voir la note thématique « Corruption ».

Il est crucial d'écouter les deux parties d'un différend. Les insensés tirent des conclusions hâtives et se précipitent pour juger, tandis que les sages prennent le temps nécessaire pour comprendre pleinement la situation.

Le sort : Tirer au sort était une méthode reconnue pour chercher la volonté de Dieu ( **Nb 27.21** ; **Jos 18.6-10** ). L’Éternel, dans sa souveraineté, déterminait le résultat du sort ( **Pr 16.33** ).

18:19 A brother offended is harder to be won than a strong city,.... A fortified city may sooner be taken by an enemy, than one brother offended can be reconciled to another; their resentments against each other are keener than against another person that has offended them; and their love being turned into hatred, it is more bitter; and it is more difficult to compose differences between brethren than between enemies; wherefore such should take care that they fall not out by the way: this is true of brethren in a natural sense;

18:20 A man's belly shall be satisfied with the fruit of his mouth,.... With his own words and discourses, when they are prudent, pious, and savoury; when they are with grace, and minister it; they are satisfying to himself, to his own mind and conscience, and to his family; to all within his house, which is, as it were, his belly; but, if otherwise, it will not be profitable nor satisfying to either; and therefore, if a man would keep conscience easy, and be useful to others, he ought to take care what he says;
La mort et la vie : Les sages parlent avec prudence ( **10.19** ; **11.12** ; **12.23** ) car ils comprennent que les mots ont des conséquences profondes ( **13.3** ; **14.3** ).

18:22 Whoso findeth a wife,.... A good one; so the Septuagint, Vulgate Latin, Syriac, and Arabic versions, supply it; and so the Targum, though it leaves out the word good in the last clause; and no other can be meant, even a good natured one, wise, prudent, careful, and industrious; a proper helpmeet, a virtuous woman, as in Pro 31:10; whoso seeks after such an one, and finds one, especially one that has the grace of God, which he should seek after among his friends, and by their assistance, and by prayer to God: findeth a good thing;
Dieu punira les puissants qui méprisent les pauvres ; En revanche, les sages prennent soin des nécessiteux ( **11.24** ; **28.27** ; **29.7** , **14** ).

Certaines personnes qui se présentent comme des « amis » ne le sont en réalité pas. Les sages, cependant, vont au-delà des apparences et savent reconnaître un véritable ami d’un faux ami en observant leur comportement dans des situations difficiles.
